coredata.dc:title: A red emissive covalently bonded graphene quantum dot-Tetraaminophenylporphyrin hybrid as a probe for the sensing of L-proline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Pallikkara, Athira
Vijay, Pothoppurathu M.
Ramakrishnan, Kala
Jayasree, Elambalassery G.
coredata.dc:description: L-Proline, the exclusive secondary amino acid with proteinogenic properties, is a vital contributor to body metabolism and the maintenance of muscle repairs, playing a pivotal role in numerous physiological processes in the human body. However, the current understanding of proline sensing is limited. In this study, we have used Tetraaminophenylporphyrin (TAPP) covalently functionalized graphene quantum dot (GQD) as a probe for the selective and sensitive detection of L-Proline. Analytical studies carried out under the optimized conditions reveal a good linear range of 9.8×10−5 M to 3.5×10−3 M with 1.6×10−7 M limit of detection. Photophysical studies reveal a ground state complex formation between the probe and proline which is responsible for the turn-off behaviour. This is not present in the case of the individual components GQD and TAPP separately which highlights the importance of covalent functionalization. The stabilization energy of the complex between the probe and different aminoacids and the corresponding hydrogen bond distances between these two moieties were calculated theoretically using density functional theory. This corroborates the enhanced stability of complex formation and the selectivity of probe towards proline compared to other aminoacids. The practical utility of the developed probe for the detection of proline in real samples like commercial milk and artificial urine is also demonstrated by spike recovery analysis which results in a recovery percent and RSD values within the acceptable limit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Porphyrins
Proline
Sensor
Density functional theory

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical procedure for the determination of L-Pro
Optimization of different parameters for sensing
Unveiling the sensing mechanism by computational calculations

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Cochin University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Cochin University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Cochin University of Science and Technology (CUSAT) for the support through SMNRI project and Department of Physics, CUSAT for various analysis. Also this work obtained partial financial support from TDP/BDTD/34/2021, RUSA and PLEASE projects. The authors would also like to thank the computer centre at the Centre of Integrated Studies, CUSAT for providing the computational facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence response of TAPP-NHCO-GQD in the presence of L-Pro.

Optimization of different parameters for sensing (A) the degree of fluorescence quenching of 5.6 ppm TAPP-NHCO-GQD on treating with 2.5×10−3 M L-Pro in acidic, neutral & basic pH from 1 to 12, (B) the degree of fluorescence quenching of various concentrations of TAPP-NHCO-GQD in the range of 2.5 ppm to 10 ppm on treating with 2.5×10−3 M L-Pro at pH of 7.2 and (C) Time versus fluorescence intensity plot in which black dots represent the probe intensity & red dots represent the probe intensity in presence of L-Pro at pH of 7.2.
(A) Variations in the fluorescence response of TAPP-NHCO-GQD in the presence of incremental addition of L-Pro from 0 mM to 3 mM at pH of 7.2 (B) Calibration plot of fluorescence intensity.

(A) Selectivity of TAPP-NHCO-GQD towards L-Pro as compared to other amino acids, represented as bar diagram, at pH of 7.2 (B) Interference study shows the fluorescence response of TAPP-NHCO-GQD in presence of different coexisting species along with fixed concentration of L-Pro in 1:100 ratio, at pH of 7.2.

(A) Changes in the average lifetime value of TAPP-NHCO-GQD by the incremental addition of proline via Time Correlated Single Photon Counting technique (B) Stern-Volmer plot of changes in fluorescence (F0/F) or average lifetime (τ0/τ) versus various concentrations of proline (C) Changes in the UV–Visible spectra of TAPP-NHCO-GQD by the incremental addition of proline and (D) Benesi-Hildebrand plot for the calculation of binding constant of TAPP-NHCO-GQD & proline.
Optimized geometries and stabilization energies of parents and covalent system complexes with proline.
Optimized geometries of probe (TAPP-NHCO-GQD) with all other interfering amino acids and their corresponding prominent hydrogen bond distances between the probe and acid/amine groups of amino acids.
Comparison of the performance of different probes reported earlier towards proline detection.
Determination of L-Pro in real samples such as pretreated milk and artificial urine using the probe, TAPP-NHCO-GQD.*

head.ce:title: A red emissive covalently bonded graphene quantum dot-Tetraaminophenylporphyrin hybrid as a probe for the sensing of L-proline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi-22, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi-22, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inter University Centre for Nanomaterials and Devices, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi-22, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inter University Centre for Nanomaterials and Devices
Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Inter University Centre for Nanomaterials and Devices, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi-22, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene Quantum Dot-Tetraaminophenylporphyrin based covalent system as a fluorescent sensor platform for the detection of L-Proline with low LOD, response time and selectivity.

L-Proline, the exclusive secondary amino acid with proteinogenic properties, is a vital contributor to body metabolism and the maintenance of muscle repairs, playing a pivotal role in numerous physiological processes in the human body. However, the current understanding of proline sensing is limited. In this study, we have used Tetraaminophenylporphyrin (TAPP) covalently functionalized graphene quantum dot (GQD) as a probe for the selective and sensitive detection of L-Proline. Analytical studies carried out under the optimized conditions reveal a good linear range of 9.8×10−5 M to 3.5×10−3 M with 1.6×10−7 M limit of detection. Photophysical studies reveal a ground state complex formation between the probe and proline which is responsible for the turn-off behaviour. This is not present in the case of the individual components GQD and TAPP separately which highlights the importance of covalent functionalization. The stabilization energy of the complex between the probe and different aminoacids and the corresponding hydrogen bond distances between these two moieties were calculated theoretically using density functional theory. This corroborates the enhanced stability of complex formation and the selectivity of probe towards proline compared to other aminoacids. The practical utility of the developed probe for the detection of proline in real samples like commercial milk and artificial urine is also demonstrated by spike recovery analysis which results in a recovery percent and RSD values within the acceptable limit.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: L-Proline (L-Pro) is one of the biologically important amino acid and the only natural cyclic one that contains a secondary amine group. In all living systems like animals and plants, proline is a crucial component of protein synthesis, structure and metabolism (especially the production of arginine, polyamines, and glutamate via pyrroline-5-carboxylate).[1,2,3] It is the most abundant amino acid in milk proteins due to its high requirement for neonatal growth and development.[4] It also plays essential roles in immunological responses, antioxidative processes, and anti-inflammatory responses. In the case of animals, proline is necessary for promoting tissue repair and nitrogen balance with wounds and burns.[5] Besides, proline acts as a signalling molecule, a sensor of cellular energy status, and a source of pyrroline-5-carboxylate (P5C) and superoxide anion, which participate in redox reactions in the animal body.[6,7] Whereas in the case of plants, proline metabolism can influence programmed cell death of plant tissues, having protective or scavenging features, as well as it stabilizes the cellular homeostasis during stress conditions.[8].

Excess proline in blood leads to the state of Hyperprolinemia, where as its deficiency may prevent optimal connective tissue maintenance. Hyperprolinemia is an inherited metabolic disorder of proline metabolism. It is characterized by abnormally high levels of proline which is usually 3–10 times (type I) and 10–15 times (type II) higher than the normal range (99–363 μmol/L) in the blood and urine. It exhibits seizures, intellectual disability or other neurological or psychiatric problems.[9] Therefore the sensing of proline is a prime one. Some of them includes electrophoretic and chromatographic techniques combined with fluorescence, ultraviolet, mass spectrometry and electrogenerated chemiluminescence detections have been reported for the detection of proline. However, the current reports on proline sensing is limited and the existing approaches are complicated and expensive.[10] Among the various sensing techniques fluorescent sensors possess high sensitivity, high specificity, immune to light scattering, ease of operation and accuracy.[11,12] In this scenario, the designing of synthetic fluorescent sensing systems for the detection of L-Pro in the biological samples has been a significant research topic.

Graphene Quantum dots (GQDs) have created enormous scientific interest as a unique fluorescent material from the carbon nano-familydue to their outstanding characteristics and application possibilities.[13] Unlike two-dimensional graphene, it has a non-zero bandgap with unique electronic and photophysical properties which areattributed to quantum confinement[14] and the edge effect.[15] GQDs have many advantageous material properties such as tunable luminescence, photobleaching resistance, low toxicity, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, high stability, better solubility, high electrical and thermal conductivity as compared to the conventional inorganic semiconductor quantum dots and other organic fluorophores.[16,17] Also these metal-free quantum dots find possible applications in the field of optoelectronics, photovoltaics, sensors,[18,19] bioimaging,[20] and so on. Even though, they face certain limitations such as low quantum yield, poor conductivity[21,22,23] due to the presence of surface/trap states[24] which are possible to be addressed by the method of functionalization. GQD functionalization alters its physical properties by bringing changes in the photoluminescence, reducing the vibrational relaxation,[25] bandgap tuning and so on.[26,13,21] A number of organic/inorganic molecules are available for this phenomenon, among them functionalization with porphyrin moieties is noteworthy. Because porphyrin molecules have intriguing features such as high molar extinction coefficients, thermal and photostability, and functionalization is simple due to the increased accessibility of sites.[27].

The reports based on the covalent combination of GQD and porphyrin is rare, howbeit there are a number of reports having non-covalent interactions within the systems. Mandal et al. made a theoretical study on the electronic states of porphyrin sensitized GQDs and postulate their practical utility as a photosensitizer in DSSCs.[28] To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt on this form of nanohybrids. Later, Jokazi et al. developed covalent and non-covalent systems of manganese tetra 4-aminophenyl porphyrin (MnTAPP) with GQDs.[29] Among the two the covalent one, MnTAPP@GQDs shows a better electrocatalytic activity towards the sensing of hydrazine as compared to the other, eventhough both of them shows good selectivity.

In light of this, here we have synthesized Tetraaminophenylporphyrin covalently functionalized GQD (TAPP-NHCO-GQD) in which TAPP & GQD are connected through amide (–NHCO-) linkage. This material has been utilized as a probe for the selective and sensitive detection of L-Pro. Analytical studies were initiated by the selective detection of probe towards proline as compared to other interfering amino acids. Further the different sensing parameters such as concentration of the probe, pH of the medium and response time were optimized. As compared to other proline sensing reports, this work shows a better linear range and limit of detection (LOD) value. Absorption spectroscopy, steady state fluorescence and decay studies reflect that the sensing follows a static quenching mechanism, via the stable complex formation between TAPP-NHCO-GQD and proline. Computational calculations were carried out in support to experimental observations and the practical applicability of this probe was analyzed.

Chemicals and solvents of analytical reagent grade used for the synthesis of aminoporphyrin and graphene quantum dots, are purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Spectrochem, TCI chemicals and Alfa Aesar. Analytical studies were carried out using HPLC grade solvents. UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded by Spectro UV–Vis Double beam UVD-3500 spectrometer, in the range of 200–900 nm. Horiba Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure all the fluorescence parameters for sensing and Horiba DeltaHub (source: 360 nm nanoLED) used for the fluorescence decay studies. The corresponding lifetime data were analyzed by the exponential curve fitting with the aid of DAS6 software. The synthesis and characterization of TAPP-NHCO-GQD has been reported in our earlier work.[30] This material has been utilized for the sensing of L-Pro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical procedure for the determination of L-Pro
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solution of TAPP-NHCO-GQD was prepared by dissolving 10 mg of the material in 10 ml acetonitrile solvent. Further the desired concentrations of probe were prepared by diluting this stock solution. All the solutions of proline and other interfering amino acids were prepared in double distilled water with a concentration of 5 mM. Selectivity and interference studies were done using various analytes such as Arginine (Arg), Cysteine (Cys), Glycine (Gly), Histidine (His), Leucine (Leu), Methionine (Met), Phenylalanine (Phe), Threonine (Thr), Tryptophan (Try) and Valine (Val). For interference studies, solutions of these analytes were added to 5.6 ppm of TAPP-NHCO-GQD and 2.5 mM L-Pro, followed by measuring the emission spectra at 658 nm. Concentration study was carried out using the same concentration of TAPP-NHCO-GQD as above to which an incremental addition of analyte was done in the concentration ranging from 0 to 3.7 mM. All the fluorometric titrations were recorded at room temperature. The same procedure of concentration study was repeated for the lifetime analysis with & without proline and the plot was developed with counts against thetime of decay.

Commercial milk samples and artificial urine was used as the real samples for examining the practical utility of our probe. The purchased milk was pretreated to remove most of the calcium, lipid micelles & lipophilic vitamins according to the earlier reported procedures.[31] After that pH was adjusted to 6.8–7.5 and used for the analysis. Similarly the artificial urine was prepared as per reported procedures, with a pH of 5.5–6.5.[32] These samples were spiked with three different concentrations (0.5, 1 & 1.5 mM) of standard L-Pro solutions under optimized conditions and fluorescence was recorded repeatedly for five times. The corresponding recovery percentage, relative standard deviation (precision) and relative percentage error (accuracy) were calculated with the aid of calibration plots.[33].

All the computational data were obtained from density functional theory (DFT) calculations by the method of CAM-B3LYP and 6-31G(d,p) basis set, using Gaussian 09 program package. The initial geometries of TAPP, GQD (13 ring membered structure with a number of –COOH & –OH groups)[34] and TAPP-NHCO-GQD were designed by the GaussView 5.0 software corresponding to their molecular weights obtained and they were fully optimized without any symmetry constraints. The geometries of each of the amino acid complexes with TAPP-NHCO-GQD were optimized and subjected to study at the same level of theory.

Tetraaminophenylporphyrin covalently functionalized GQD (TAPP-NHCO-GQD) is synthesized from Tetraaminophenylporphyrin (TAPP) and GQDs by the one pot synthesis.[35] The synthesis and characterization of this covalent system is reported in our earlier work.[30] Persuaded by its fluorescence properties after functionalization, we have used it as a probe for the sensing of L-Pro in this study. UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy, steady state fluorescence and decay studies were executed to evaluate the developed probe.

The sensing of proline is initiated by the changes in the fluorescence intensity of TAPP-NHCO-GQD at 658 nm in the presence of L-Pro. Fig. 1 shows the fluorescence intensity of the probe in the absence (navy) and presence (cyan) of proline, reflecting the turn-off behaviour of the probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of different parameters for sensing
ce:section.ce:para: With the intention of getting an effective sensing we have optimized the different conditions such as pH, time and concentration of probe. For the pH, the fluorescence response of TAPP-NHCO-GQD with proline was evaluated in media like acidic, neutral & basic. Acetate buffer (pH 2.1, 4.3, 5.6), phosphate buffer (pH 7.2) and tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.4, 9.2, 10, 11.9) were used to acquire the required pH. Among these conditions the highest quenching efficiency (QE) was exhibited by neutral pH at 7.2 (Fig. 2 A). Thusly, further studies were conducted under this pH. While optimizing the concentration of probe, we have chosen a range from 2.5 to 10 ppm prepared from the stock solution of TAPP-NHCO-GQD (1000 ppm) in acetonitrile. Fixed concentration of proline (2.5×10−3 M) was added to these probe solutions at neutral pH and the fluorescence QE was recorded, which is shown in Fig. 2B. It is discerned that 5.6 ppm probe gives the best QE that is considered as the optimum concentration of probe for the upcoming studies.

The stability of the probe and response time was evaluated by recording the fluorescence intensity of probe with and without proline at regular time intervals. Initially, the effect of irradiation time on TAPP-NHCO-GQD was evaluated and it was noted that the fluorescence intensity remains the same up to 10 min indicating the stability of the probe (black dots in Fig. 2C). After adding 2.5×10−3 M L-Pro, the intensity quenches immediately and retained the same up to next 10 min (red dots in Fig. 2C). Thusly, TAPP-NHCO-GQD’s quick response towards proline is useful for the real-time monitoring of samples.

The efficacy of the probe towards sensing of proline was evaluated by the concentration study in which the optimized parameters were used as the sensing conditions. Fig. 3 A shows the regular quenching of the fluorescence of TAPP-NHCO-GQD on the incremental addition of L-Pro (0 to 3×10−3 M) at pH of 7.2. The degree of fluorescence quenching is 83% corresponding to the 3.7×10−3 M concentration of L-Pro. The calibration plot (Fig. 3B) showed linearity in the range 9.8×10−5 M to 3.5×10−3 M, with a correlation coefficient of 0.995. Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated using the following Eqs. (1) and (2) respectively,
(1) LOD = 3S/m
(2) LOQ = 10S/m
where S is the standard deviation of the fluorescence measurements of the blank (6 times measurement) and m is the slope of the calibration line.[36] The values are 1.6×10−7 M and 5.5×10−7 M for LOD and LOQ respectively. The Stern-Volmer (SV) plot was drawn with respect to the fluorescence quenching (Fig. 5B), which manifests a positive deviation from linearity (concave towards y-axis) indicating the chances of combined static and dynamic quenching. This was further verified by lifetime analysis.

Both selectivity and interference studies are important parameters in sensor applications since the analyte has to be analyzed in the real samples which may contain a number of molecules other than the target one. In order to pursue these studies, the fluorescence intensity of the probe in presence of similar concentration (2.5×10−3 M) of proline and other amino acids such as Arginine (Arg), Cysteine (Cys), Glycine (Gly), Histidine (His), Leucine (Leu), Methionine (Met), Phenylalanine (Phe), Threonine (Thr), Tryptophan (Try) and Valine (Val) were monitored separately, at pH of 7.2. The fluorescence of TAPP-NHCO-GQD has no momentous change (Fig. 4 A) by the addition of the amino acids other than L-Pro that indicates the good selectivity of our probe towards proline.

For the interference study, the fluorescence response of 5.6 ppm of TAPP-NHCO-GQD, with the coexisting species in the presence of fixed concentration of L-Pro (2.5×10−3 M), in three different molar ratios such as 1:1, 1:10 & 1:100 were recorded at pH of 7.2. Fig. 4B shows that there exists a well tolerance of our probe towards the coexisting species even on 100 fold excess concentration. Both the selectivity and interference studies shows that TAPP-NHCO-GQD is highly selective towards L-Pro as compared to other amino acids.

When proline is present, the fluorescence of the probe diminishes, signaling the turn-off behaviour of the sensor and sensing inevitably involves predicting its mechanism. When an analyte is present, a system can interact in a variety of ways such as ground state interactions[37] (complex formation, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic/π-π interactions), excited state interactions[38,39] (electron/energy transfer) or may be due to inner filter effect[40,36]. In our sensor, there is no overlapping between the emission spectra of TAPP-NHCO-GQD and absorption spectra of L-Pro (Fig. S1). This observation ruled out the chances of Forster Resonance Energy Transfer between the probe and analyte. Likewise, the absorption spectrum of L-Pro neither overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of the TAPP-NHCO-GQD indicating the absence of inner filter effect as well.[41].

Lifetime measurements of the probe were performed with and without the addition of L-Pro to explore the quenching behaviour and hence the underlying mechanism of recognition. The average lifetime (τavg) values of the probe, obtained from time correlated single photon counting, stays consistent throughout the gradual addition of proline (Fig. 5 A) and their corresponding fitting parameters are given as Table S1. The average lifetime of probe without L-Pro (τavg = 3.3×10−11 s) and with 3.7×10−3 M L-Pro (τavg = 2.9×10−11 s) exhibits no characteristic changes except some potential instrumental errors. Additionally, their corresponding SV plot is a horizontal line of the form y = 1 (Fig. 5B). These inferences excluded the plausibility of dynamic quenching mechanism due to photoinduced electron transfer between the TAPP-NHCO-GQD and L-Pro.

Consequently, the possibility of static quenching due to the ground state interactions remain within the systems. Static quenching, on the other hand, may occur as a result of the creation of a complex between the fluorophore with the quencher. It also exclusively impacts the excited states of fluorophores. In this situation, the lifetime of the probe will stay constant even after the addition of an analyte, and τ0/τ = 1, where τ0 and τ are the lifetime of the probe without and with analyte respectively. Herein, the spectroscopic data show the presence of a static kind of quenching and the development of a non-emissive ground state complex. To gain a better understanding of the kind of quenching, we carefully examined the UV–Visible absorption spectra of the probe and (probe + L-Pro) complex at 420 & 270 nm. Fig. 5C manifests the absorption spectra in which the absorbance of TAPP-NHCO-GQD quenches regularly on increasing the concentration of L-Pro. This is due to the effective interaction between probe and L-Pro in its ground state like hydrogen bonding/electrostatic interactions, matching with the static quenching process for our proposed sensor.

Eventually, Fig. 5D shows the Benesi-Hildebrand (B-H) plot from which the binding constant of TAPP-NHCO-GQD with proline was calculated using the B-H equation (Eqs. S1) which is about 6.23×103 M−1. Thusly our developed probe, TAPP-NHCO-GQD act as a fluorescent sensor for the selective detection of L-Proline via static quenching mechanism due to ground state complex formation.

The newly designed probe for proline sensing was compared to the previously reported sensors which are tabulated in Table 1 . The majority of the proline sensors based on these reported ones were found to be paper-based sensors upon analysis. A fluorescent supramolecular assembly was designed by Sierra et al. for the detection of L-Pro,[42] wherein the authors have described the binding of L-Pro with fluorescent cavitands, but the corresponding analytical values are not included. Only limited number of reports are available on this analyte, among which our probe shows a better LOD and measurement range.

The fluorescence response of parent systems, GQD and TAPP were evaluated in order to understand the effect of covalently functionalized probe. This study was conducted by taking the same concentration of both GQD and TAPP equivalent to that of the probe. On adding 2.5×10−3 M concentration of proline to these systems there observed a quenching of 3.35% and 3.93% respectively for GQD and TAPP, as depicted in Fig. S2. However, the QE in TAPP-NHCO-GQD is about 53.57 % for the same concentration of proline, which indicates the effect of covalent functionalization towards the sensing of L-Pro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unveiling the sensing mechanism by computational calculations
ce:section.ce:para: All the experimental observations and plausible static quenching mechanism were further explained via DFT calculations by CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level of theory. This was done by obtaining the optimized geometry of TAPP, GQD, TAPP-NHCO-GQD & amino acids under study with minimum energy, as depicted in Fig. S3. As the IR spectrum indicates the presence of –NH2 group along with amide bond formation,[34] we have chosen one of the fragment of TAPP-NHCO-GQD as a model of the covalent system (Fig. 6 ) and studied the similar interactions possible for all the amino acids including proline. All further calculations were based on these geometries. Fig. 6 shows the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) surface of TAPP-NHCO-GQD, which manifests an effective charge separation within the probe molecules that succors them to form stable complexes with L-Pro.

The geometries of GQD:Pro, TAPP:Pro & TAPP-NHCO-GQD:Pro complexes were optimized at the same level of theory (Fig. 7 ). The energy of these optimized geometries were extracted and the corresponding stabilization energy (SE) was calculated. The observed SEs are in the order TAPP:Pro (-6.36 kcal/mol) < GQD:Pro (-12.18 kcal/mol) < TAPP-NHCO-GQD:Pro (-20.06 kcal/mol). The exceptional high SE of probe:proline complex than the parent systems is in agreement with the effect of functionalization observed experimentally.

Further, to address the selectivity of probe towards L-Pro we have analyzed the interaction of the probe with all other interfering amino acids, such as Arg, His, Lys, Met, Thr, Val, Leu, Phe, Try and Gly. The optimized geometries of the corresponding complexes are given in Fig. 8 and their calculated SEs are tabulated in Table S2. The structures given in Fig. 8 shows that proline could exactly fit to the TAPP-NHCO-GQD probe interacting with the GQD as well as TAPP portion of the probe with two possible N-H and O-H hydrogen bond interaction. However, this is not the case with other amino acids. These amino acids have interactions either to the TAPP or GQD portion within the probe, and not with both simultaneously. The quantitative analysis of these hydrogen bonds are also given in Fig. 8.

In TAPP-NHCO-GQD:Pro complex, the carbonyl oxygen (>C = O) of proline forms an O∙∙∙H hydrogen bond with the hydroxyl hydrogen (–OH) of GQD within the probe. At the same time, an N∙∙∙H bond exists between the amino nitrogen (–NH2) of TAPP portion of the probe and hydroxyl hydrogen (–OH) of proline. The bond distances are 1.73 Ao and 1.59 Ao respectively for N∙∙∙H and O∙∙∙H bonds and both of these values fall within the range of conventional hydrogen bonds. This is a promising result that highlights the stable ground state complex formation between the probe and L-Pro. In the case of TAPP-NHCO-GQD:Met, no characteristic intermolecular hydrogen bonding interaction was observed. In all the TAPP-NHCO-GQD:amino acid complexes other than hydrogen bonding other favorable non-covalent interactions such as H∙∙∙C interaction, and π-π interaction are also present, which may also contribute to the stabilization energy of these complexes. Meanwhile, the remaining interfering amino acids possess either N∙∙∙H or O∙∙∙H interactions with the probe. Therefore the selective sensing of TAPP-NHCO-GQD towards L-Pro and plausible mechanism of sensing, as well as the effect of functionalization of TAPP & GQD are very much supported by these computational calculations.

The practical feasibility of the developed sensor towards the analysis of proline in real samples were perused by spike recovery method.[48] Pretreated milk and artificial urine are used as the real samples which were prepared by the reported procedures as discussed in the experimental section 2.3. The analysis was carried out in the optimized conditions and the recovery percentage, relative standard deviation (RSD) & accuracy values for each addition were calculated. The results shown in Table 2 reflect that, the recovery percent is much closer to 100% and RSD & accuracy values less than 4%, which is promising for the utility of this method for the rapid detection of L-Pro in milk and urine samples and satisfied the ICH guidelines.

In essence, GQD-Tetraaminophenylporphyrin based easy and economical turn-off fluorescence sensor has been developed for the sensitive and selective determination of fundamental amino acid L-Pro. The fluorometric titrations reveal that the covalent functionalized probe is found to be more sensitive towards L-Pro than the GQD & TAPP separately. Besides, the functionalization improves the selectivity, stability & disaggregation of porphyrins as a fluorescent probe. In case of TAPP-NHCO-GQD, the fluorescence intensity varied linearly in the range 9.8×10−5 M to 3.5×10−3 M with a detection limit of 1.6×10−7 M and quantification limit of 5.5×10−7 M. Mechanism behind the sensing shows a static quenching due to the ground state complex formation between the probe and analyte, acquired from UV–Visible absorption and steady state decay studies.

DFT calculations on the TAPP-NHCO-GQD and its corresponding non-covalent systems with amino acids hold up the experimental observations. High stability of the complex, TAPP-NHCO-GQD:Pro (-20 kcal/mol) as compared to that with other interfering amino acids as well as the complexes of the proline with the parent systems, theoretically proves the sensitivity and selectivity of the covalently functionalized system. The sensing of proline in spiked commercial milk and artificial urine ensures the reliability of developed probe for the real sample analysis. The probe developed in the present study, TAPP-NHCO-GQD is of paramount importance because this detection is rapid, facile, cost-effective and has good measurement range & high sensitivity than the earlier reported ones that opens up the chances of developing new probes.

Athira Pallikkara: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Pothoppurathu M. Vijay: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kala Ramakrishnan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Elambalassery G. Jayasree: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Cochin University of Science and Technology (CUSAT) for the support through SMNRI project and Department of Physics, CUSAT for various analysis. Also this work obtained partial financial support from TDP/BDTD/34/2021, RUSA and PLEASE projects. The authors would also like to thank the computer centre at the Centre of Integrated Studies, CUSAT for providing the computational facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115367.

ce:section.ce:para:
